summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Eric Raymond's frustrations with UNIX printing usability by arguing that the UNIX community is culturally incapable of prioritizing usability. He contends that despite Raymond's well-intentioned call for better UI design, the open source community's foundation in kernel hacking, C code, and command-line tools makes usability a foreign concern. Atwood asserts that usability is harder than writing an operating system kernel because it requires understanding actual user behavior. He concludes that meaningful usability improvements will come from Microsoft and Apple, companies that have cultivated cultures respecting usability, rather than from the open source world.
key_points: UNIX's cultural foundation in technical excellence and command-line tools makes usability adoption fundamentally incompatible with its community values
Usability is an order of magnitude harder than writing technically competent code, even harder than writing an OS kernel
The open source community has had thirty years to produce a usable GUI and history suggests the next five years won't be different
Good UI design requires understanding what users actually do versus what they say they do, which conflicts with open source developers' preference for 'the fun stuff'
Companies like Microsoft and Apple will lead usability improvements because they've cultivated cultures that respect it
money_quotes: Nobody gives a damn how technically competent your code is when they can't figure out how to use it. Without usability, you have nothing.
Usability is easily an order of magnitude harder than writing technically competent code, even harder than writing your own operating system kernel.
The fact that this mess is an example of our best in action, rather than our worst, just highlights how appallingly low our standards have been.
It is comically unrealistic to ask a community predicated on C code, kernel hacking, and the utility of command line tools, to suddenly wake up and get the usability religion.
Open-source developers don't have time for things that are a pain in the ass – like users, their conflicting needs, and their general disdain for computers and technology.
Good UI design is not a result of black magic, it just requires paying attention.
key_insight: Cultural values, not technical capability, determine whether a software community can prioritize usability—and UNIX's hacker culture fundamentally conflicts with user-centered design.
